patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 00/10] nfsd: a pile of fixes for random bugs

version_history.subject: [PATCH 00/10] nfsd: a pile of fixes for random bugs

series_items.subject: [PATCH 01/10] nfsd: fix BUG_ON in nfsd4_alloc_layout_stateid on racing delegation revoke
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a race condition in the NFSv4 server (nfsd) where a concurrent delegation revoke can cause a BUG_ON when trying to read fi_deleg_file without holding the corresponding lock. The fix involves taking the spinlock around the fi_deleg_file read and replacing the BUG_ON with a graceful error return.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the spinlock usage, suggesting it may not be necessary due to the recent commit that added a stability guarantee. Also pointed out a potential issue with rcu_dereference_protected() and suggested adding a comment.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 29 May 2026, Jeff Layton wrote:
> nfsd4_alloc_layout_stateid reads fp->fi_deleg_file without holding
> fi_lock when the parent stateid is a delegation. A concurrent delegation
> revoke via the laundromat can clear fi_deleg_file under fi_lock, causing
> nfsd_file_get() to return NULL and triggering the BUG_ON.
> 
> This race is client-reachable: two NFS clients can trigger it by having
> one hold a delegation while another opens the same file to force a
> recall. When the first client doesn't respond to the recall, the
> laundromat revokes it. A concurrent LAYOUTGET from any client using the
> delegation stateid hits the race window.
> 
> Fix this by taking fi_lock around the fi_deleg_file read in the
> SC_TYPE_DELEG path, and replacing the BUG_ON with a graceful error
> return that cleans up the partially-initialized layout stateid.

Replacing the BUG_ON with a graceful error is certainly sensible and
probably all that is needed to fix the problem.

I cannot see how the spinlock achieves anything.  If ->fi_deleg_file
could become NULL at this point, it can become NULL just before we take
the spinlock.

We do need to be sure the file (if there is one) doesn't get freed while
nfsd_file_get() is incrementing the refcount, but rcu_read_lock() is the
normal tool for that.
In this case we have

  		ls->ls_file = nfsd_file_get(rcu_dereference_protected(fp->fi_deleg_file, 1));

rcu_dereference_protected(...., 1)
which for me is a warning sign.  What does the '1' mean here?
Presumably something that we cannot easily assert with a C condition, in
which case a comment is called for.

Based on the recent commit which added this I'm guessing 
   fi_delegees > 0 guarantees stability

If that is right, then why do we also need a spinlock to guarantee
stability?

Confused.

NeilBrown

> 
> Fixes: c5c707f96fc9 ("nfsd: implement pNFS layout recalls")
> Assisted-by: kres:claude-opus-4-7
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  fs/nfsd/nfs4layouts.c | 12 +++++++++---
>  1 file changed, 9 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs4layouts.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs4layouts.c
> index 9ed2e3d65062..5d48c7673fa1 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4layouts.c
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4layouts.c
> @@ -247,11 +247,17 @@ nfsd4_alloc_layout_stateid(struct nfsd4_compound_state *cstate,
>  	nfsd4_init_cb(&ls->ls_recall, clp, &nfsd4_cb_layout_ops,
>  			NFSPROC4_CLNT_CB_LAYOUT);
>  
> -	if (parent->sc_type == SC_TYPE_DELEG)
> +	if (parent->sc_type == SC_TYPE_DELEG) {
> +		spin_lock(&fp->fi_lock);
>  		ls->ls_file = nfsd_file_get(rcu_dereference_protected(fp->fi_deleg_file, 1));
> -	else
> +		spin_unlock(&fp->fi_lock);
> +	} else {
>  		ls->ls_file = find_any_file(fp);
> -	BUG_ON(!ls->ls_file);
> +	}
> +	if (!ls->ls_file) {
> +		nfs4_put_stid(stp);
> +		return NULL;
> +	}
>  
>  	ls->ls_fenced = false;
>  	ls->ls_fence_delay = 0;
> 
> -- 
> 2.54.0
> 
>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 02/10] nfsd: drain callbacks and clear cl_cb_session
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server (nfsd) where a session is freed while an RPC task still holds a reference to it, causing a use-after-free error. The patch introduces a two-stage drain mechanism in nfsd4_probe_callback_sync() to wait for all RPC tasks to complete before freeing the session. Additionally, clp->cl_cb_session is cleared unconditionally on error returns from nfsd4_process_cb_update(). This ensures that the NFS server properly handles session teardown and callback processing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/10] nfsd: serialize nfsd4_end_grace() with atomic test-and-set
series_items.patch_summary: This patch serializes the nfsd4_end_grace() function by using an atomic test-and-set operation to ensure that only one thread can transition the grace_ended flag from false to true at a time, preventing concurrent access and corruption of the reclaim_str_hashtbl list.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 04/10] nfsd: dedup nfs4_client_to_reclaim inserts
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFSv4 client recovery mechanism where duplicate entries were being inserted into the reclaim hash table, leading to incorrect behavior during the grace period. The fix involves computing the string hash value first and then looking up the name in the hash table. If an existing entry is found, the new principal hash is folded into the existing record without allocating a new entry or modifying the hash table size. This ensures that the fast end-of-grace path works correctly and prevents dangling entries from being left in the hash table.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 05/10] nfsd: gate nfs3 setacl by argp->mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the nfsd3_proc_setacl function to gate NFSv3 setacl operations based on the argp->mask value. The function previously called set_posix_acl unconditionally for both ACL_TYPE_ACCESS and ACL_TYPE_DEFAULT, but now checks the corresponding mask bit before setting the ACLs. This change ensures that a SETACL with mask=NFS_ACL silently drops the directory's default ACL, and a request with neither bit set leaves the on-disk ACLs untouched.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/10] NFSD: Enable return of an updated stable_how to NFS clients
series_items.patch_summary: This patch enables the return of an updated stable_how value to NFS clients, which will be used in a subsequent patch to promote UNSTABLE WRITEs to FILE_SYNC WRITEs, reducing round trips and allowing clients to discard cached dirty data. The patch refactors nfsd_vfs_write() to return a possibly modified stable_how value without changing its behavior. It affects several files in the NFS server code, including nfs3proc.c, nfs4proc.c, nfsproc.c, vfs.c, and xdr3.h.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/10] NFSD: check truncate permission under inode lock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a TOCTOU (Time-of-Check, Time-of-Use) vulnerability in the NFSD code by ensuring that truncate permission checks are performed under inode lock. The patch splits the write-count acquisition from the truncation permission check and rechecks whether the requested size is below the locked file size after acquiring the inode lock. This change prevents concurrent appends from extending the file before the NFSD MAY_TRUNC check, which rejects append-only files. The patch also avoids loading the unlocked inode->i_size value.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 08/10] nfsd: fix partial-write detection in nfsd_direct_write
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd_direct_write function, which is responsible for handling direct writes to NFS servers. The issue was that partial-write detection was not working correctly, allowing short writes to complete between 50% and 99% of the segment size without breaking the loop. This caused the next segment's payload to be written at the wrong offset and over-reporting the count to the NFS client. To fix this, a snapshot of the segment's byte count is taken before writing, and host_err is compared against this snapshot instead of the original request length. This ensures that any short write breaks the loop as expected.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 09/10] nfsd: cap decoded POSIX ACL count to bound sort cost
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents a potential denial of service attack on the NFS server by capping the count of decoded POSIX ACL entries at a fixed maximum, rather than allowing it to be chosen by the client. The problem arises from an O(n^2) bubble sort used in the server's compound processing path, which can lead to unbounded CPU usage if the client chooses an excessively large count. By rejecting counts greater than NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES before any allocation or sorting occurs, the patch bounds the number of comparisons and prevents the potential denial of service attack.

review_comments.author: Rick Macklem
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Rick Macklem noted that Chuck previously disagreed with limiting the number of ACEs to NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES, arguing it was specific to NFSv3 ACL protocol and the RPC message size limit was sufficient; Rick personally agrees with a limit of 1024 but invites Chuck's input.
review_comments.raw_body:
My recollection is that Chuck didn't like this limit. He argued that it was
specific to the NFSv3 ACL protocol and that the limit on the size of a NFSv4
RPC message was sufficient.  I, personally, think that 1024 is a reasonable
limit for # of ACEs, but Chuck can jump in here if he doesn't agree.
(Note that, if user/groups are configured as "#s in the string", a lot of ACEs
fits in a 4Mbyte RPC message and the server file system will also set a limit
although, as you note, that happens after the sort.)

The good news w.r.t. the sort is that they are normally already sorted,
so the bubble sort is normally just a single pass, I think?

rick

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that using NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES to cap the number of ACEs in NFSv4 ACLs introduces technical debt, as this limit is not defined by the NFSv4 protocol itself and should be clearly distinguished from a protocol limit. He suggested defining an implementation-specific constant with a clear name, such as NFSD4_MAX_yada, to indicate it's an NFSD constraint rather than a protocol requirement.
review_comments.raw_body:
The NFSACL protocol limits the number of ACEs in an ACL to NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES
(1024). It’s a limit that is defined in the protocol itself.

The NFSv4 protocol sets no similar limit. In particular, NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES
is not a constant defined or used by the NFSv4.x family of protocols IIRC.

Using NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES to cap the number of ACEs in NFSv4 ACLs is a
convenience, but it adds technical debt (slight though it may be).

So… We can define an implementation limit for NFSv4 ACL support in NFSD.
But it shouldn’t be called NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES, IMHO. For clarity of
documentation, pick a number (could be 1024) and state in a comment that
it is an NFSD implementation constraint, not a protocol limit. Name the
constant something like NFSD4_MAX_yada to make it clear it is an
implementation limit, not a protocol limit.

(Check if the Linux POSIX ACL implementation already defines and imposes
a similar limit before defining a new one for NFSv4).

Then prepare for an onslaught of complaints that 1024 is just too small :-)


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever suggested an alternative approach to capping ACL entry counts, proposing that they be capped at the same number as those set via NFSACL, and requested a clear explanation for why this is done.
review_comments.raw_body:
A different take on this might be that we want to ensure that ACLs set
via the NFSv4 POSIX ACL extension can always be retrieved via NFSACL.
In that case, capping the ACE count at the same number makes sense.
As long as the reason for this is clearly mentioned.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH 10/10] nfsd: validate symlink target length in NFSv4 CREATE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a security vulnerability in the NFSv4 CREATE operation by validating the length of symlink targets to prevent a client from forcing an unbounded kmalloc allocation. The patch rejects oversized targets and returns an error code early, preventing the allocation from occurring. This fix addresses a long-standing issue that was introduced in Linux 2.6.12-rc2.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses various bugs and issues in the NFS daemon, including remotely-triggerable panics, use-after-free errors, denial-of-service vulnerabilities, and protocol fixes. The patches were categorized as 'random bugs' but are now being addressed collectively. The set passes basic pynfs smoke testing and includes contributions from multiple authors.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a race condition in nfsd4_alloc_layout_stateid, agreed to fix it by taking fi_lock around the fi_deleg_file read and replacing BUG_ON with a graceful error return.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about a potential use-after-free bug in the nfsd4 callback handling code, specifically that the per-session teardown path can free a session while rpciod still holds an inflight callback. The author agrees to mirror the two-stage drain already performed by nfsd4_shutdown_callback() and clear clp->cl_cb_session unconditionally on error returns.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author Chris Mason pointed out a concurrency issue in nfsd4_end_grace() where two callers can observe grace_ended == false and proceed into nfsd4_record_grace_done(), leading to list corruption and double-free of nfs4_client_reclaim entries. The author agreed that this is a bug, explained the fix using an atomic test-and-set with try_cmpxchg(), and promised to apply it in v2.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>

nfsd4_end_grace() guards its drain path with a plain bool:

    if (nn->grace_ended)
            return;
    nn->grace_ended = true;

The read and the write are independent, and nothing in struct
nfsd_net serializes them.  At least two contexts can reach this
code with no lock held:

    laundromat path
      laundry_wq kworker
        nfs4_laundromat()
          nfsd4_end_grace()

    RECLAIM_COMPLETE path
      nfsd compound kthread
        nfsd4_reclaim_complete()
          inc_reclaim_complete()
            nfsd4_end_grace()

Both callers can observe grace_ended == false on different CPUs,
both store true, and both proceed into nfsd4_record_grace_done(),
which invokes the active client_tracking_ops->grace_done callback.
For tracking ops that drain reclaim_str_hashtbl (legacy_tracking_ops
via nfsd4_recdir_purge_old, and the cld v1+ ops via
nfsd4_cld_grace_done), grace_done calls nfs4_release_reclaim(),
which walks every bucket of reclaim_str_hashtbl with no lock and
calls nfs4_remove_reclaim_record() (list_del + kfree) on each
entry.  Two concurrent walkers corrupt the list and double-free
every nfs4_client_reclaim.  A concurrent nfsd4_find_reclaim_client()
iterating the same bucket reads through freed memory.

A third call site exists in nfs4_state_start_net() on the
skip_grace startup path, but it runs under nfsd_mutex before any
client has connected and before the laundromat's first delayed
work fires, so it cannot race with the two callers above.

Fix by replacing the read/write pair with try_cmpxchg() so exactly
one caller transitions grace_ended from false to true and proceeds
into the drain; the loser returns immediately.  bool supports
1-byte cmpxchg on all supported architectures, and no lock
ordering changes are needed.

Fixes: 362063a595be ("nfsd: keep a tally of RECLAIM_COMPLETE operations when using nfsdcld")
Assisted-by: kres:claude-opus-4-7
Signed-off-by: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>
---
 fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c | 17 ++++++++++++++---
 1 file changed, 14 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c
index f4d12dbcf97b..dc4ac541436f 100644
--- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c
+++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c
@@ -7022,12 +7022,23 @@ nfsd4_renew(struct svc_rqst *rqstp, struct nfsd4_compound_state *cstate,
 static void
 nfsd4_end_grace(struct nfsd_net *nn)
 {
-	/* do nothing if grace period already ended */
-	if (nn->grace_ended)
+	bool expected = false;
+
+	/*
+	 * nfsd4_end_grace() can be entered concurrently from the
+	 * laundromat workqueue and from an nfsd compound thread
+	 * handling RECLAIM_COMPLETE.  Without serialization, both
+	 * callers can observe grace_ended==false and proceed into
+	 * nfsd4_record_grace_done().  For tracking ops whose
+	 * grace_done drains reclaim_str_hashtbl, that results in
+	 * list corruption and a double free of every
+	 * nfs4_client_reclaim entry.  Use an atomic test-and-set so
+	 * exactly one caller proceeds.
+	 */
+	if (!try_cmpxchg(&nn->grace_ended, &expected, true))
 		return;
 
 	trace_nfsd_grace_complete(nn);
-	nn->grace_ended = true;
 	/*
 	 * If the server goes down again right now, an NFSv4
 	 * client will still be allowed to reclaim after it comes back up,

-- 
2.54.0

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the nfs4_client_to_reclaim() function causing a memory leak and incorrect behavior when folding in new principal hashes into existing reclaim records. The author agreed to restructure the code in v2 to fix these issues, including pairing the stores of cr_princhash.data and .len with smp_store_release() and smp_load_acquire() respectively.
sentiment_signals: acknowledged a need for a fix

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern that nfsd3_proc_setacl() silently drops the directory's default ACL when the client doesn't send it, and promised to fix this by adding mask checks before calling set_posix_acl().
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>

nfsd3_proc_setacl() calls set_posix_acl() unconditionally for both
ACL_TYPE_ACCESS and ACL_TYPE_DEFAULT, passing argp->acl_access and
argp->acl_default verbatim. The NFSv3 ACL decoder only populates
those pointers when the corresponding mask bit is set:

    nfs3svc_decode_setaclargs()
      if (args->mask & NFS_ACL)    decode into acl_access
      if (args->mask & NFS_DFACL)  decode into acl_default
      /* otherwise the pointer stays NULL (pc_argzero) */

    nfsd3_proc_setacl()
      set_posix_acl(.., ACL_TYPE_ACCESS,  argp->acl_access)
      set_posix_acl(.., ACL_TYPE_DEFAULT, argp->acl_default)

set_posix_acl(idmap, dentry, type, NULL) is the VFS "remove this
ACL type" operation. A NULL pointer that means "the client did not
send this arm" is therefore indistinguishable from "the client
asked to remove this ACL". A SETACL with mask=NFS_ACL silently
drops the directory's default ACL; mask=0 drops both.

The sibling nfsd3_proc_getacl() already consults argp->mask before
touching each arm; mirror that in setacl.

Fix by wrapping each set_posix_acl() call in the matching mask bit
check and initializing error to 0 before inode_lock so that a
request with neither bit set leaves the on-disk ACLs untouched and
returns nfs_ok. The out_drop_lock path and the unconditional
posix_acl_release() at out: are preserved; both NULL-tolerate the
skipped arms.

Fixes: a257cdd0e217 ("[PATCH] NFSD: Add server support for NFSv3 ACLs.")
Assisted-by: kres:claude-opus-4-7
Signed-off-by: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>
---
 fs/nfsd/nfs3acl.c | 17 +++++++++++------
 1 file changed, 11 insertions(+), 6 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs3acl.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs3acl.c
index e87731380be8..a87f9d7f32be 100644
--- a/fs/nfsd/nfs3acl.c
+++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs3acl.c
@@ -105,12 +105,17 @@ static __be32 nfsd3_proc_setacl(struct svc_rqst *rqstp)
 
 	inode_lock(inode);
 
-	error = set_posix_acl(&nop_mnt_idmap, fh->fh_dentry, ACL_TYPE_ACCESS,
-			      argp->acl_access);
-	if (error)
-		goto out_drop_lock;
-	error = set_posix_acl(&nop_mnt_idmap, fh->fh_dentry, ACL_TYPE_DEFAULT,
-			      argp->acl_default);
+	error = 0;
+	if (argp->mask & NFS_ACL) {
+		error = set_posix_acl(&nop_mnt_idmap, fh->fh_dentry,
+				      ACL_TYPE_ACCESS, argp->acl_access);
+		if (error)
+			goto out_drop_lock;
+	}
+	if (argp->mask & NFS_DFACL) {
+		error = set_posix_acl(&nop_mnt_idmap, fh->fh_dentry,
+				      ACL_TYPE_DEFAULT, argp->acl_default);
+	}
 
 out_drop_lock:
 	inode_unlock(inode);

-- 
2.54.0

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the nfsd_direct_write() function not properly detecting short writes, which can lead to incorrect file offsets and over-reporting of written bytes. The author agrees that the issue exists and proposes fixing it by snapshotting the segment's byte count before writing and comparing it with the actual error returned by vfs_iocb_iter_write().

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the nfsd_vfs_write() function, which was modified to return a possibly modified stable_how value to its callers. The author explained that this change indicates the client does not need a subsequent COMMIT operation, saving a round trip and allowing the client to dispense with cached dirty data as soon as it receives the server's WRITE response.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the decoder introduced in commit 5fc51dfc2eb1 omitted a symmetric check for ACL entry count, agreed to reject wire counts greater than NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES before allocation to bound CPU usage.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>

nfsd4_decode_posixacl() reads a u32 entry count off the wire and passes
it straight to posix_acl_alloc() and sort_pacl_range(). The latter is
an O(n^2) bubble sort, so a client-chosen count drives unbounded CPU in
the server's compound processing path.

    nfsd4_decode_posixacl()
      xdr_stream_decode_u32(&count)       /* uncapped u32 */
      posix_acl_alloc(count, GFP_KERNEL)
      sort_pacl_range(*acl, 0, count - 1) /* O(n^2) bubble sort */

The encoder side in the same file already rejects ACLs whose a_count
exceeds NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES, but the decoder introduced in commit
5fc51dfc2eb1 ("NFSD: Add support for XDR decoding POSIX draft ACLs")
omitted the symmetric check.

Fix by rejecting a wire count greater than NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES with
nfserr_resource, before any allocation, so the sort is bounded by
NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES^2 comparisons.

Fixes: 5fc51dfc2eb1 ("NFSD: Add support for XDR decoding POSIX draft ACLs")
Assisted-by: kres:claude-opus-4-7
Signed-off-by: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>
---
 fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c | 2 ++
 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+)

diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c
index c6c50c376b23..5469c6c207ba 100644
--- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c
+++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c
@@ -448,6 +448,8 @@ nfsd4_decode_posixacl(struct nfsd4_compoundargs *argp, struct posix_acl **acl)
 
 	if (xdr_stream_decode_u32(argp->xdr, &count) < 0)
 		return nfserr_bad_xdr;
+	if (count > NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES)
+		return nfserr_resource;
 
 	*acl = posix_acl_alloc(count, GFP_KERNEL);
 	if (*acl == NULL)

-- 
2.54.0

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about a TOCTOU window in nfsd_setattr() for append-only files, agreeing to split the write-count acquisition from the truncation permission check and recheck whether the requested size is below i_size_read(inode) after inode_lock() has been acquired. The patch will need further revision.

review_comments.author: Rick Macklem
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Rick Macklem noted that Chuck Lever previously argued against limiting the number of ACEs to 1024 in NFSv3 ACL protocol, suggesting it was specific to this protocol and that the size limit on NFSv4 RPC messages was sufficient. The reviewer personally agrees with a limit of 1024 but invites Chuck to disagree.
review_comments.raw_body:
My recollection is that Chuck didn't like this limit. He argued that it was
specific to the NFSv3 ACL protocol and that the limit on the size of a NFSv4
RPC message was sufficient.  I, personally, think that 1024 is a reasonable
limit for # of ACEs, but Chuck can jump in here if he doesn't agree.
(Note that, if user/groups are configured as "#s in the string", a lot of ACEs
fits in a 4Mbyte RPC message and the server file system will also set a limit
although, as you note, that happens after the sort.)

The good news w.r.t. the sort is that they are normally already sorted,
so the bubble sort is normally just a single pass, I think?

rick

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: The reviewer, Chuck Lever, pointed out that the patch incorrectly uses a constant defined in the NFSACL protocol (NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES) to cap the number of ACEs in NFSv4 ACLs, which is not a protocol limit. He suggested defining an implementation-specific limit for NFSD and naming it clearly as such, rather than reusing the existing constant.
review_comments.raw_body:
The NFSACL protocol limits the number of ACEs in an ACL to NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES
(1024). It’s a limit that is defined in the protocol itself.

The NFSv4 protocol sets no similar limit. In particular, NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES
is not a constant defined or used by the NFSv4.x family of protocols IIRC.

Using NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES to cap the number of ACEs in NFSv4 ACLs is a
convenience, but it adds technical debt (slight though it may be).

So… We can define an implementation limit for NFSv4 ACL support in NFSD.
But it shouldn’t be called NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES, IMHO. For clarity of
documentation, pick a number (could be 1024) and state in a comment that
it is an NFSD implementation constraint, not a protocol limit. Name the
constant something like NFSD4_MAX_yada to make it clear it is an
implementation limit, not a protocol limit.

(Check if the Linux POSIX ACL implementation already defines and imposes
a similar limit before defining a new one for NFSv4).

Then prepare for an onslaught of complaints that 1024 is just too small :-)


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Reviewer NeilBrown questioned the necessity of a spinlock in nfsd_file_get(), suggesting that rcu_read_lock() would be sufficient to prevent file freeing while incrementing refcount, and asked for clarification on the '1' parameter in rcu_dereference_protected().
review_comments.raw_body:
Replacing the BUG_ON with a graceful error is certainly sensible and
probably all that is needed to fix the problem.

I cannot see how the spinlock achieves anything.  If ->fi_deleg_file
could become NULL at this point, it can become NULL just before we take
the spinlock.

We do need to be sure the file (if there is one) doesn't get freed while
nfsd_file_get() is incrementing the refcount, but rcu_read_lock() is the
normal tool for that.
In this case we have

  		ls->ls_file = nfsd_file_get(rcu_dereference_protected(fp->fi_deleg_file, 1));

rcu_dereference_protected(...., 1)
which for me is a warning sign.  What does the '1' mean here?
Presumably something that we cannot easily assert with a C condition, in
which case a comment is called for.

Based on the recent commit which added this I'm guessing 
   fi_delegees > 0 guarantees stability

If that is right, then why do we also need a spinlock to guarantee
stability?

Confused.

NeilBrown

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that capping the decoded POSIX ACL count to prevent excessive sort cost could be justified if it's clearly documented, but suggested considering an alternative approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
A different take on this might be that we want to ensure that ACLs set
via the NFSv4 POSIX ACL extension can always be retrieved via NFSACL.
In that case, capping the ACE count at the same number makes sense.
As long as the reason for this is clearly mentioned.


-- 
Chuck Lever

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix XDR length calculation in nfsd4_ff_encode_layoutget

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix XDR length calculation in nfsd4_ff_encode_layoutget

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the XDR (External Data Representation) length calculation in nfsd4_ff_encode_layoutget(). The function incorrectly calculates the buffer size, leading to potential out-of-bounds writes or memory leaks. The fix involves breaking down each encoded field explicitly and using xdr_align_size() for variable-length opaque fields.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified the bug and suggested a fix. The patch was found to be correct and was applied to the nfsd-testing branch.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

On Thu, 28 May 2026 10:38:15 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> The XDR buffer size calculation in nfsd4_ff_encode_layoutget() has
> multiple errors that can result in either an out-of-bounds write or
> leaking uninitialized kernel memory to the client:
> 
>  - fh_len doesn't account for XDR padding on the file handle data
>  - uid and gid lengths use "8 + len" but xdr_encode_opaque() actually
>    writes "4 + xdr_align_size(len)" bytes
>  - ds_len omits the flags and stats_collect_hint fields (8 bytes),
>    while len's header constant overestimates by 8 bytes -- these
>    partially cancel but leave a net mismatch
> 
> [...]

Applied to nfsd-testing, thanks!

[1/1] nfsd: fix XDR length calculation in nfsd4_ff_encode_layoutget
      commit: c8bb9d5360bfb534065aed091579199ad2843f43

--
Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix XDR padding calculation in ff_encode_getdeviceinfo

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix XDR padding calculation in ff_encode_getdeviceinfo

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server's XDR (External Data Representation) padding calculation for the GETDEVICEINFO reply. The issue is that the declared length of the da_addr_body field exceeds the actual encoded data by 2-8 bytes, causing stale reply-page content to leak to the client and mis-aligning subsequent version list decodes. The patch uses xdr_align_size() to match the actual encoding of string lengths.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the patch's potential impact on other parts of the codebase, specifically the UID/GID string calculation in nfsd4_ff_encode_layoutget(). Suggested converting to xdrgen instead, but acknowledged that this would be a larger and less back-portable change.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, May 27, 2026, at 2:30 PM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> nfsd4_ff_encode_getdeviceinfo() computes the da_addr_body reservation
> as 16 + netid_len + addr_len, but the subsequent xdr_encode_opaque()
> calls emit 8 + round_up(netid_len, 4) + round_up(addr_len, 4) bytes.
> The mismatch means the declared da_addr_body length exceeds the actual
> encoded data by 2-8 bytes on every flexfile GETDEVICEINFO reply,
> leaking stale reply-page content to the client and mis-aligning the
> subsequent version list decode.
>
> Use xdr_align_size() for each string length to match what
> xdr_encode_opaque() actually writes.
>
> Fixes: efcae97fa425 ("NFSD: da_addr_body field missing in some 
> GETDEVICEINFO replies")
> Assisted-by: kres:claude-opus-4-6
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  fs/nfsd/flexfilelayoutxdr.c | 3 ++-
>  1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
>
> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/flexfilelayoutxdr.c b/fs/nfsd/flexfilelayoutxdr.c
> index f9f7e38cba13..7f357dbd1bb1 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/flexfilelayoutxdr.c
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/flexfilelayoutxdr.c
> @@ -94,7 +94,8 @@ nfsd4_ff_encode_getdeviceinfo(struct xdr_stream *xdr,
>  	}
> 
>  	/* len + padding for two strings */
> -	addr_len = 16 + da->netaddr.netid_len + da->netaddr.addr_len;
> +	addr_len = 8 + xdr_align_size(da->netaddr.netid_len) +
> +		   xdr_align_size(da->netaddr.addr_len);
>  	ver_len = 20;
> 
>  	len = 4 + ver_len + 4 + addr_len;
>
> ---
> base-commit: b69fc3eaa867d0caa904634ea7a1b4569411b163
> change-id: 20260527-pnfs-fixes-23451bb03d57
>
> Best regards,
> -- 
> Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

Fixes tag might/should be 9b9960a0ca47 ? LLM reviews vary on this.

nfsd4_ff_encode_layoutget at fs/nfsd/flexfilelayoutxdr.c:40-41 has the
same unaligned pattern for UID/GID strings: 8 + uid.len + 8 + gid.len.
Do you intend to address that calculation as well?

My preference here is to convert to xdrgen instead, but that's a much
bigger and less back-portable change.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Applied the patch to nfsd-testing and thanked the author for the fix.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

On Wed, 27 May 2026 14:30:41 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> nfsd4_ff_encode_getdeviceinfo() computes the da_addr_body reservation
> as 16 + netid_len + addr_len, but the subsequent xdr_encode_opaque()
> calls emit 8 + round_up(netid_len, 4) + round_up(addr_len, 4) bytes.
> The mismatch means the declared da_addr_body length exceeds the actual
> encoded data by 2-8 bytes on every flexfile GETDEVICEINFO reply,
> leaking stale reply-page content to the client and mis-aligning the
> subsequent version list decode.
> 
> [...]

Applied to nfsd-testing, thanks!

[1/1] nfsd: fix XDR padding calculation in ff_encode_getdeviceinfo
      commit: d53136757d4192934ad67d4c5c58eb9bc99daf4b

--
Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH 0/6] Harden server-side RPCSEC GSS decode and backchannel teardown

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] fuse: disable default bdi strictlimiting

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/9] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 0/8] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru
[PATCH v5 0/9] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm: list_lru: fix set_shrinker_bit() call during race with cgroup deletion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a long-standing bug in the list_lru code, where the shrinker bit is set on the wrong cgroup when there's a race between list_lru_add() and cgroup deletion. The fix involves returning the 'effective' memcg from the locking function and having the caller use it to set the shrinker bit.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch without any specific comments or concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 27/05/2026 21:45, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> When list_lru_add() races with cgroup deletion, the shrinker bit is set
> on the wrong group and lost. This can cause a shrinker run to miss the
> cgroup that actually has the object.
> 
> When the passed in memcg is dead, the function finds the first non-dead
> parent from the passed in memcg and adds the object there; but the
> shrinker bit is set on the memcg that was passed in.
> 
> This bug is as old as the shrinker bitmap itself.
> 
> Fix it by returning the "effective" memcg from the locking function, and
> have the caller use that.
> 
> Fixes: fae91d6d8be5 ("mm/list_lru.c: set bit in memcg shrinker bitmap on first list_lru item appearance")
> Reported-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
> Reported-by: Sashiko
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> ---
>  mm/list_lru.c | 26 +++++++++++++++-----------
>  1 file changed, 15 insertions(+), 11 deletions(-)
> 

Acked-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks in the lock_list_lru_of_memcg() function, which is used to acquire a lock for list additions and deletions. The function will not fail to acquire the lock, so these checks are no longer needed. This change simplifies the code and makes it more efficient.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the unlock_list_lru() function by consolidating its implementation into a single inline function, eliminating duplicate code in both MEMCG and !MEMCG variants. The function is responsible for unlocking the lock associated with a list_lru_one structure, taking into account whether interrupts are disabled or not. This change simplifies the codebase and reduces maintenance overhead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the check for a race condition between list LRU operations and cgroup deletion to the caller of lock_list_lru_of_memcg(). This is done by removing the dead list check from the function itself, allowing for future unification of the lock_list_lru() variants. The change affects only the MEMCG variant of lock_list_lru(), which needs to handle this specific race condition.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the lock_list_lru() function by moving it to a separate static inline function and removing its duplicate definition in the code. The shared function is used in both MEMCG and non-MEMCG paths, reducing code duplication and improving maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API. It provides normal and irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the addition and deletion functions to allow callers to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state. This is necessary because some users, such as the THP shrinker, require this synchronization to maintain consistency. The patch adds new locking functions and modifies existing ones to support this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new helper function called folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() that allocates list_lru heads for shrinkable folios under the memory cgroup (memcg) they are associated with. The function is designed to handle the complexities of accessing the memcg of a folio, which requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. It aims to make the existence check lightweight under RCU and only pin the memcg when necessary for allocation. This patch is part of a larger series that prepares the THP shrinker to use list_lru, and it has been reviewed by several kernel developers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] mm: memory: flatten alloc_anon_folio() retry loop
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the alloc_anon_folio() function by flattening its retry loop to improve code readability and maintainability. The original top-level if statement was buried four levels deep, making it difficult to understand and modify. By moving the success path to a more visible location, the code becomes cleaner and easier to work with. This change is made in preparation for additional code that will be added in the next patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change addresses a suboptimal handling of cgroups in the deferred split queue, where large pages on other nodes were being split unnecessarily. The new approach streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used by other major shrinkers already. The patch also adds calls to create node heads for the cgroup at once when splittable pages are created, eliminating the need for special care in migration code between nodes.

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a folio is split across multiple nodes, and requested that the list_lru per-memcg heads be instantiated on demand when the first object of interest is allocated for a cgroup.

Reviewer SeongJae Park reported that the patch causes a build failure in UM mode due to an implicit function declaration error, specifically for the folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() function when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is unset. They suggested defining the function for this case and provided a temporal fix as evidence.

Reviewer SeongJae Park noted that the patch is missing a definition for folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE is unset, causing an implicit function declaration error in UM mode kunit tests.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Johannes,

On Wed, 27 May 2026 16:45:16 -0400 Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:

---

I found this patch is now in mm-new and it makes UM mode kunit fails like
below.

    $ ./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --kunitconfig mm/damon/tests/
    [00:00:02] Configuring KUnit Kernel ...
    [00:00:02] Building KUnit Kernel ...
    Populating config with:
    $ make ARCH=um O=.kunit olddefconfig
    Building with:
    $ make all compile_commands.json scripts_gdb ARCH=um O=.kunit --jobs=8
    ERROR:root:../mm/swap_state.c: In function \u2018__swap_cache_alloc\u2019:
    ../mm/swap_state.c:468:26: error: implicit declaration of function \u2018folio_memcg_alloc_deferred\u2019 [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
      468 |         if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
          |                          ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    make[4]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:289: mm/swap_state.o] Error 1
    make[4]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[3]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:548: mm] Error 2
    make[3]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[2]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:2143: .] Error 2
    make[1]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2
    make: *** [Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2

Maybe we can define the function for CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE unset case?  I
confirmed the below attaching temporal fix works for at least kunit.


Thanks,
SJ

[...]
=== >8 ===

---

From: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
Date: Wed, 27 May 2026 23:58:07 -0700
Subject: [PATCH] mm/huge_mm: define memcg_alloc_deferred() for
 !CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE
MIME-Version: 1.0
Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
Content-Transfer-Encoding: 8bit

Without this, UM mode kunit fails like below.

    $ ./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --kunitconfig mm/damon/tests/
    [00:00:02] Configuring KUnit Kernel ...
    [00:00:02] Building KUnit Kernel ...
    Populating config with:
    $ make ARCH=um O=.kunit olddefconfig
    Building with:
    $ make all compile_commands.json scripts_gdb ARCH=um O=.kunit --jobs=8
    ERROR:root:../mm/swap_state.c: In function \u2018__swap_cache_alloc\u2019:
    ../mm/swap_state.c:468:26: error: implicit declaration of function \u2018folio_memcg_alloc_deferred\u2019 [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
      468 |         if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
          |                          ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    make[4]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:289: mm/swap_state.o] Error 1
    make[4]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[3]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:548: mm] Error 2
    make[3]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[2]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:2143: .] Error 2
    make[1]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2
    make: *** [Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2

Fix by implementing the function for CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE unset
case.

Fixes: https://lore.kernel.org/20260527204757.2544958-10-hannes@cmpxchg.org
Signed-off-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
---
 include/linux/huge_mm.h | 5 +++++
 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+)

diff --git a/include/linux/huge_mm.h b/include/linux/huge_mm.h
index f6c2531a27a35..055de7b8ed487 100644
--- a/include/linux/huge_mm.h
+++ b/include/linux/huge_mm.h
@@ -663,6 +663,11 @@ static inline int folio_split(struct folio *folio, unsigned int new_order,
 	return -EINVAL;
 }
 
+static inline int folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(struct folio *folio)
+{
+	return 0;
+}
+
 static inline void deferred_split_folio(struct folio *folio, bool partially_mapped) {}
 #define split_huge_pmd(__vma, __pmd, __address)	\
 	do { } while (0)
-- 
2.47.3

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer questioned the necessity of incrementing count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK) in a specific scenario, suggesting either removing it or modifying the control flow to avoid this increment
review_comments.raw_body:
Ah sorry, should have caught this in the previous version, do we need

count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK);

here?

or maybe we just goto next instead of goto fallback and trty next
viable order?

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed Usama Arif's concern that an unexpected failure of a sub-costly-order slab allocation could be missed by the event counter, explaining that such failures are rare and would already be logged in dmesg.

Author addressed a concern about the naming convention for the list_lru allocation, agreeing that 'fallback' is more correct and choosing to leave the code as is.
review_comments.raw_body:
This isn't an allocation we expect to fail with any sort of routine
that we'd need to capture it in the event counter. It would warn in
dmesg if it did. But in practice it can't happen at all, since it's a
sub-costly-order slab allocation and the allocator would loop and OOM
kill stuff until it succeeds.

---

Again I don't think it matters, but fallback seems a bit more correct
because the size of the list_lru allocation doesn't change with lower
orders (until we hit 0).

So I think we can just leave it as is.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a mistake in their testing and promised to add UM (User Mode) builds to their final compile test before resubmitting the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Whoops, thanks for the fix, SJ. I'll incorporate UM builds into my
final compile test before sending.

Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series, version 5, aims to switch the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of the current implementation. The changes include fixing a long-standing issue in set_shrinker_bit() where the bit can be set on a dying child memcg instead of the ancestor that received the item. Other changes include adapting to the new lock_list_lru_of_memcg() signature, propagating folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() failure, and fixing a use-after-free bug in deferred_split_isolate(). The patches have been rebased onto the latest mm-unstable tree.

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary:
SeongJae Park noted that the patch does not handle the case where a THP is being split and its child folios are still on the list_lru, which can lead to a use-after-free error when the parent folio is freed.

Reviewer SeongJae Park noted that the patch causes a build failure in UM mode due to an implicit function declaration error for folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is unset, and suggested defining the function for this case.

SeongJae Park noted that without the definition of folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() for !CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE, UM mode kunit fails due to an implicit function declaration error in __swap_cache_alloc(), and provided a patch to define this function.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Usama Arif questioned the necessity of counting MTHP statistics for anon fault fallbacks, suggesting either removing the count or modifying the logic to skip the fallback and try the next viable order.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ah sorry, should have caught this in the previous version, do we need

count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK);

here?

or maybe we just goto next instead of goto fallback and trty next
viable order?

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed Usama Arif's concern that the event counter should capture failures of folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(), explaining that such failures are rare and would be logged in dmesg, but acknowledged that a fix is needed to handle this edge case.

Author addressed a concern about the naming convention for the fallback list_lru allocation, agreeing that 'fallback' is more correct and choosing to leave the code as is.
review_comments.raw_body:
This isn't an allocation we expect to fail with any sort of routine
that we'd need to capture it in the event counter. It would warn in
dmesg if it did. But in practice it can't happen at all, since it's a
sub-costly-order slab allocation and the allocator would loop and OOM
kill stuff until it succeeds.

---

Again I don't think it matters, but fallback seems a bit more correct
because the size of the list_lru allocation doesn't change with lower
orders (until we hit 0).

So I think we can just leave it as is.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a mistake in their testing and agreed to add UM (User Mode) builds to their final compile test before resubmitting the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Whoops, thanks for the fix, SJ. I'll incorporate UM builds into my
final compile test before sending.

Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v5 9/9] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Deferred split shrinker switched to list_lru allocation

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the allocation failure handling and fallback mechanism, but ultimately decided it's not a significant issue

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch and gave an Acked-by tag, indicating approval
review_comments.raw_body:
On 28/05/2026 15:02, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> On Thu, May 28, 2026 at 02:32:06PM +0100, Usama Arif wrote:
>>
>>
>> On 27/05/2026 21:45, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>>> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
>>> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
>>> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
>>> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
>>>
>>>         alloc/unmap
>>>           deferred_split_folio()
>>>             list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
>>>             set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>>>
>>>         for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
>>>           mem_cgroup_iter()
>>>             shrink_slab(node, memcg)
>>>               shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
>>>                 if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>>>                   deferred_split_scan()
>>>                     walks memcg->split_queue
>>>
>>> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
>>> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
>>> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
>>>
>>> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
>>> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
>>> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
>>> queue as well.
>>>
>>> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
>>> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
>>> folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
>>> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
>>>
>>> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
>>> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
>>>
>>> Reported-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>
>>> Tested-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>
>>> Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
>>> Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
>>> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
>>> ---
>>>  include/linux/huge_mm.h    |   7 +-
>>>  include/linux/memcontrol.h |   4 -
>>>  include/linux/mmzone.h     |  12 --
>>>  mm/huge_memory.c           | 364 +++++++++++++------------------------
>>>  mm/internal.h              |   2 +-
>>>  mm/khugepaged.c            |   5 +
>>>  mm/memcontrol.c            |  12 +-
>>>  mm/memory.c                |   4 +
>>>  mm/mm_init.c               |  15 --
>>>  mm/swap_state.c            |  10 +
>>>  10 files changed, 150 insertions(+), 285 deletions(-)
>>>
>>
>> [...]
>>
>>> diff --git a/mm/memory.c b/mm/memory.c
>>> index 135f5c0f57bd..f22e61d8c8de 100644
>>> --- a/mm/memory.c
>>> +++ b/mm/memory.c
>>> @@ -5222,6 +5222,10 @@ static struct folio *alloc_anon_folio(struct vm_fault *vmf)
>>>  			folio_put(folio);
>>>  			goto next;
>>>  		}
>>> +		if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
>>> +			folio_put(folio);
>>
>> Ah sorry, should have caught this in the previous version, do we need
>>
>> count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK);
>>
>> here?
> 
> This isn't an allocation we expect to fail with any sort of routine
> that we'd need to capture it in the event counter. It would warn in
> dmesg if it did. But in practice it can't happen at all, since it's a
> sub-costly-order slab allocation and the allocator would loop and OOM
> kill stuff until it succeeds.
> 
>> or maybe we just goto next instead of goto fallback and trty next
>> viable order?
> 
> Again I don't think it matters, but fallback seems a bit more correct
> because the size of the list_lru allocation doesn't change with lower
> orders (until we hit 0).
> 
> So I think we can just leave it as is.

Ack!

Acked-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
